coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer fluorescent polymer dots without conventional chromophores: Synthesis, emission mechanism and applications as Cu2+ probe and fluorescent ink
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Yunqian
Zhang, Hao
Cao, Duxia
Chen, Qifeng
Guan, Ruifang
Zhou, Chuanjian
coredata.dc:description: Recently, the non-aromatic fluorescent materials without benzene rings in their structures have aroused widespread interest. In this work, a citric acid functionalized polyethyleneimine oligomer, CA-PEI, was synthesized through acyl chloride method. In CA-PEI, the polyethyleneimine was bonded to citric acid molecule with a molar ratio of 1:1 through an amide bond, which was confirmed by NMR, FTIR, GPC and XPS. The results indicated that CA-PEI had dual excitation and dual emission properties, and the two emission centers were derived from n–σ* and n–π* transition, respectively. CA-PEI was used to synthesize the light-emitting polymer dots N-PDs through a hydrothermal reaction. Interestingly, compared with CA-PEI, N-PDs had dual excitation and single emission properties, and its Stoke's shift was as high as 193 nm. This can be attributed to the compact structure of N-PDs, which promoted the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process. Importantly, N-PDs can serve as excellent probes in response to Cu2+, with a detection limit as low as 2.5 nmol L−1. The response mechanism involves the formation of a copper amine complex between Cu2+ and the PEI part of N-PDs, which then quenches the fluorescence emission of N-PDs through the fluorescence inner-filter effect. N-PDs as well were applied as an encryptable fluorescent ink.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Emission mechanism
Non-aromatic fluorescent materials
Ion probe
Fluorescence internal filter effect
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Electronic transition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General procedure for probing metal ions
Preparation and characterization of CA-PEI and N-PDs
Fluorescence properties of CA-PEI and N-PDs
Discussion on the luminescence mechanism of CA-PEI and N-PDs
Concentration dependence and stability of N-PDs
Response mechanism of N-PDs to copper ion
Detection of copper ions in real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation process of CA-PEI and N-PDs.

(a) 1H NMR of PEI 600, CA-PEI and N-PDs; (b) FTIR of PEI 600, CA-PEI and N-PDs; (c) GPC of CA-PEI and N-PDs; (d) XPS of CA-PEI and N-PDs; (e) and (f) are high-resolution XPS carbon spectra of CA-PEI and N-PDs, respectively.
(a) DLS of CA-PEI and N-PDs; (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of CA-PEI and N-PDs; (c) HRTEM of N-PDs.

(a) Absorption, excitation and emission spectra of CA-PEI (1.0 mg mL−1); (b) Emission spectra of CA-PEI at different excitation wavelengths (240 nm–420 nm); (c) Absorption, excitation and emission spectra of N-PDs (2.0 μg mL−1); (d) Emission spectra of N-PDs (2.0 μg mL−1) under different excitation wavelengths (240 nm–420 nm) (Some small emission peaks caused by incomplete energy transfer are shown in the circle). (pH = 7.0).
Luminescence process and emission mechanism diagram of CA-PEI molecule.
The luminescence process and emission mechanism of N-PDs.

(a) Absorption spectra of N-PDs under different concentrations (1–32 μg mL−1); (b) Emission spectra at an excitation of 244 nm; (c) Emission and (d) excitation spectra at an excitation of 353 nm; (e) The stability of N-PDs (2.0 μg mL−1) under high sodium ion concentration, and (f) The change of fluorescence intensity of N-PDs (2.0 μg mL−1) under 365 nm UV lamp for different time. (pH = 7.0).

(a) Absorption spectra of copper ions, and the absorption, excitation and emission spectra of N-PDs before and after the addition of copper ions; (b) Photos of different molecular aqueous solutions (50.0 mg L−1) before and after adding copper ions (1.0 mmol L−1) under sunlight; (c) N-PDs fluorescence quenched by copper ions and recovered by EDTA-Na2 and biothiol. (pH = 5.0).

(a) The emission spectra of N-PDs at different pH values; (b) influence of time on the relative florescence intensity of N-PDs (2.0 μg mL−1) in the presence of 10 μmol L−1 Cu2+; (c) the fluorescence intensity of N-PDs after adding different ions with a concentration of 20.0 μmol L−1 (blue bar), the gray bar is the fluorescence intensity response after the continuous addition of Cu2+ (20.0 μM); (d) the fluorescence intensity of N-PDs after adding different concentrations (0–12.0 μmol L−1) Cu2+; (e) and (f) are the semi-logarithmic and linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of Cu2+ and N-PDs at different concentrations.

(a) Photographs of a pen filled with N-PDs ink under daylight and 365 nm ultraviolet light; (b) photographs of the words "University of Jinan" written on the paper under daylight and 365 nm ultraviolet light; (c) photographs of the luminous English words quenched by Cu2+ and then restored by EDTA-Na2 under UV light.
Performance comparison of different fluorescent sensors for the detection of Cu2+.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer fluorescent polymer dots without conventional chromophores: Synthesis, emission mechanism and applications as Cu2+ probe and fluorescent ink

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong 250022 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong, 250022 China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan 250061, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bResearch Institute of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan 250061, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yunqian Ma and Hao Zhang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, the non-aromatic fluorescent materials without benzene rings in their structures have aroused widespread interest. In this work, a citric acid functionalized polyethyleneimine oligomer, CA-PEI, was synthesized through acyl chloride method. In CA-PEI, the polyethyleneimine was bonded to citric acid molecule with a molar ratio of 1:1 through an amide bond, which was confirmed by NMR, FTIR, GPC and XPS. The results indicated that CA-PEI had dual excitation and dual emission properties, and the two emission centers were derived from n–σ* and n–π* transition, respectively. CA-PEI was used to synthesize the light-emitting polymer dots N-PDs through a hydrothermal reaction. Interestingly, compared with CA-PEI, N-PDs had dual excitation and single emission properties, and its Stoke's shift was as high as 193 nm. This can be attributed to the compact structure of N-PDs, which promoted the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process. Importantly, N-PDs can serve as excellent probes in response to Cu2+, with a detection limit as low as 2.5 nmol L−1. The response mechanism involves the formation of a copper amine complex between Cu2+ and the PEI part of N-PDs, which then quenches the fluorescence emission of N-PDs through the fluorescence inner-filter effect. N-PDs as well were applied as an encryptable fluorescent ink.

ce:section.ce:para: The non-aromatic fluorescent materials have aroused increasing researchers' attention due to their lots of advantages, such as simple preparation process, good water solubility, excellent biocompatibility, intrinsic fluorescence emission, and their wide application in the fields of cell imaging, ion probes, drug delivery and light-emitting devices [1–10]. At present, the research is still in its infancy. Although a lot of research has been carried out, the research works are numerous and messy, and there is not a unified and deep explanation for the light-emitting mechanism. Polyamidoamine (PAMAM) is one of the earliest and most widely studied non-aromatic luminescent materials [11–14]. The branched tertiary amine structure in the molecule is usually considered as the luminescent center [15–17]. However, with the deepening research, some non-aromatic luminescent materials without amino groups in the molecules have been successfully prepared. Tang and coworkers[18] prepared an alternating copolymer of maleic anhydride and vinyl acetate only containing carbonyl groups, and attributed the luminescence to the clusters of carbonyl groups. Miao [19] et al. reported an aliphatic hyperbranched polyether that did not contain any chromophore and could emit strong blue fluorescence. Du et al. [20] reported a non-conjugated fluorescent hyperbranched polycarbonate and attributed the fluorescence emission to the cluster-induced emission (CIE) mechanism. In short, there is an unclear emission mechanism to these non-aromatic fluorescent materials, which severely limits their preparation, application and further development. Therefore, conducting in-depth research is of great significance.

Cu2+ is widely present in natural environments and biological systems, which is an essential trace element and a component of many important enzymes in the organisms [6, 21, 22]. Studies have shown that the lack or excess of Cu2+ in the organism can cause serious diseases [23, 24]. Therefore, the selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ in the environment is very important. Among the many detection methods, fluorescence method has gained lots of attention due to its excellent performances, such as simple equipment and detection process, high sensitivity, short response time, good selectivity and low cost. Especially non-aromatic fluorescent materials generally have low toxicity and good water solubility, it is of great significance to develop more applications of these novel fluorescence materials such as a sensitive probe of Cu2+ in the water phase in this paper.

In this work, we first synthesized CA-PEI, an oligomer of citric acid-functionalized PEI by acyl chloride method, and then N-PDs were synthesized by hydrothermal reaction using CA-PEI as a raw material. Through comparison, the differences in molecular structure, emission mechanism and fluorescence performance of the two fluorescent materials were studied in detail. Finally, the application of N-PDs as Cu2+ sensors and encryptable fluorescent inks were also studied.

Citric acid (CA), Tricarboxylic acid (TA), thionyl chloride (TC), polyethylenimine (PEI, Mw = 600), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-Na2) and tetrahydrofuran (THF) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. UV–vis absorption spectra (Shimadzu, UV2550), fluorescence spectra (Horiba, Fluoromax-4), X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS; ESCALAB 250XI), nuclear magnetic resonance spectra (NMR, MercuryPlus-400), fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR, NICOLET-iS50) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100plus) were used to characterize and investige properties of the materials.

Preparation process of citric acid chloride (CA-Cl): In this process, THF was used as the reaction solvent. TC and CA were used as the raw materials. 1.0 g CA and 15.0 mL THF were added in a round-bottomed flask under stirring to obtain a transparent solution. Then the above solution was put in a low temperature reactor (0 °C), and 1.3 mL TC (according to a molar ratio of n CA: n TC = 1:3.3) was added. The solution was kept stirring for 12 h. After the reaction, excess TC was removed through rotary evaporation at low temperature and dark to obtain light yellow transparent viscous CA-Cl, and then it was dissolved in THF and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

Preparation process of CA-PEI: THF was used as the reaction solvent. PEI and CA-Cl were the raw materials to synthesize CA-PEI. 2.0 g PEI was dispersed into 50.0 mL THF under N2 protection and low temperature (0 °C). The THF solution containing CA-Cl was slowly added dropwise to the PEI solution under magnetic stirring, and the white precipitate was gradually produced. The white product was subjected to rotary evaporation to remove the solvent to obtain CA-PEI.

0.5 g CA-PEI was dissolved into 50 mL deionized water and heated to reflux for 8 h at 100 °C. The solution gradually changed from colorless to light yellow. And then absolute ethanol as a poor solvent was added in order to precipitate N-PDs, and the precipitate was purified by repeated washing with deionized water and absolute ethanol for 3 times. Finally, the purified N-PDs were spin-dried to obtain light yellow powders, which were kept in a 4 °C refrigerator for subsequent use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for probing metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: All fluorescence and absorption tests were conducted at room temperature and performed with deionized water as the solvent. Sodium hydroxide (0.1 mol L−1) and hydrochloric acid (0.1 mol L−1) were used to adjust the pH of N–PDs aqueous solution. Various anion and cation solutions with a concentration of 0.01 mol L−1 were prepared by using hydrochloride and sodium salt, respectively.

Ion selective detection: Deionized water was used to prepare a 2.0 μg/mL solution of N-PDs. Each time, 2.0 mL of N-PDs solution was taken, and then various ionic solutions were added to achieve a final concentration of 20.0 μmol L−1. In the presence of various ions, the fluorescence emission spectra of N-PDs solution were detected with excitation wavelength of 353 nm and excitation and emission slit width of 5 nm.

Ion sensitivity detection: 2.0 mL of N-PDs solution (2.0 μg mL−1) was taken, and the change of fluorescence emission spectra with the increase of the concentration of Cu2+ (0–12 μmol L−1) was recorded.

The actual water sample was fetched from the "Jiazi Lake" of University of Jinan. First, the river water sample was left still in dark for 12 h, and then the supernatant was filtered twice via filter paper (0.22 μm). Finally, it was sealed in a 100 mL beaker and kept in refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CA-PEI and N-PDs
ce:section.ce:para: PEI is a water-soluble cationic functional polymer with rich amino groups in its molecule and there is no fluorescence emission under normal conditions. Researchers have explored multiple methods to prepare various PEI-based fluorescent materials [25–27]. In this work, a fluorescent material with CA grafted onto the end of PEI molecule has been successfully prepared by two-step amidation reaction. First, the citric acid terminal was chlorinated at low temperature to obtain CA-Cl, and then CA-Cl reacted with PEI to obtain CA-PEI, finally hydrothermalized into a fluorescent material N-PDs (Scheme 1 ). THF was used as the solvent and CA-Cl was added slowly, the white precipitate CA-PEI was continuously produced. The covalent combination of PEI and CA to form CA-PEI was confirmed by NMR, FTIR, GPC and XPS.

In 1H NMR spectra (Fig. 1 a), it is obvious that the proton peaks of PEI molecule at 2.52 and 2.56 ppm are attributed to the methylene group on the molecular chain, while the newly appeared proton peaks of CA-PEI at 2.65–2.89 ppm in the low field region are derived from the methylene group on the CA molecule, confirming the covalent bonding between PEI and CA. In the FTIR spectra (Fig. 1b), the absorption peaks of CA-PEI at 1570 cm-1 and 1645 cm-1 are attributed to the vibration modes I and II of the amide, respectively [6, 28], indicating the formation of an amide bond between PEI and CA. The GPC analysis data of CA-PEI molecules are shown in Fig. 1c and Table S1.

The results demonstrate that CA-PEI molecules show a broad molecular distribution, with a polydispersity of 2.03, a weight average molecular weight (Mw) of 1650, and an average molecular weight (Mn) of 812. For low molecular weight oligomers, the value of Mn is generally used for research. The molecular weight of PEI used in this experiment was about 600, and Mn of CA was 192. These results confirm that each PEI molecule was grafted with only one CA molecule on average. In Fig. 1d, the CA-PEI molecule contains three elements: C, N and O. It is obvious that O element was derived from CA molecule, which also confirms the synthesis of the target product.

Hydrothermal method is one of the most commonly used methods for the synthesis of fluorescent polymer dots (PDs). In this work, we dissolved the synthesized CA-PEI molecules in an aqueous medium and heated them to prepare N-PDs and further explored their structure and fluorescence properties. Their structures and morphology were characterized by 1H NMR, FTIR, GPC, XPS, TEM and DLS.

In NMR spectrum, the chemical shift of an atom is usually closely related to the environment. The proton peaks of CA-PEI have basically no change in peak shapes after heating, but they obviously shift to the low field (Fig. 1a), which proves that the chemical environment of each proton has changed. As shown in FTIR spectra (Fig. 1b), the absorption peaks of vibrational modes I and II of amides in N-PDs are obviously stronger than those of CA-PEI, and the absorption peaks corresponding to hydroxyl and amino groups at 3160–3700 cm-1 are significantly lower. The reasons can be attributed to that the heating process causes unreacted carboxylic acid and amino functional groups to further undergo amidation reaction, reducing the amount of carboxyl and amino groups and increasing the density of amide bonds. In addition, from the high-resolution XPS carbon spectra of CA-PEI and N-PDs, it can also be found that the proportion of C–O has dropped from 56.33% to 52.50% (Fig. 1e and f), which also proves that the carboxyl group has undergone the further reaction, showing a consistent result with the FTIR. GPC results show that N–PDs have a wider molecular distribution, with a polydispersity of 1.95, Mw of 1534, and Mn of 788 (Fig. 1c and Table S1). Compared with CA-PEI, its Mn is reduced for the amidation reaction under heating in the molecule. The DLS results show (Fig. 2 a) that the average particle size of N-PDs is 1.12 nm, which is much smaller than that of CA-PEI (4.19 nm). Combined with the results of GPC, it is speculated that CA-PEI molecules with more amino or carboxyl groups are easy to form clusters, while N-PDs with more amide valence are compact.

In Fig. 2b, the absorption spectrum of CA-PEI has two obvious absorption peaks at around 233 nm and 280 nm, which are attributed to the π–π* transition caused by CO, and n–σ* transition caused by isolated chromophores such as amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl [29]. Compared with CA-PEI, the absorption peak of N-PDs at around 240 nm is significantly reduced, which indicats that the density of isolated chromophores is significantly reduced. A new obvious absorption peak emerges at 300–400 nm, which can be attributed to the n–π* transition caused by the agglomeration of amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups for further amide reaction. The absorption spectra results are consistent with the results of NMR, FTIR and XPS. The HRTEM characterization shows that N-PDs with a particle size of 1.0–2.0 nm were successfully prepared (Fig. 2c), which is consistent with the DLS results (Fig. 2a). Importantly, the DLS results show that the particle sizes of the three groups of N-PDs solutions with different concentration (20.0, 2.0, and 0.2 μg/mL) are all 1.12 nm (Fig. S1). As a result, fluorescent N-PDs have been successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties of CA-PEI and N-PDs
ce:section.ce:para: Next, the same method was used to study the basic fluorescent properties of N-PDs in detail. As shown in Fig. 3c, the absorption peak at around 210 nm can be attributed to the π–π* transition, the absorption peak at around 240 nm is due to n–σ* transition, and the absorption band centered at 360 nm derives from n–π* transition. Since the absorption band produced by π–π* transition is lower than the test limit of the instrument, its fluorescence will not be discussed in detail here. Compared with CA-PEI, the absorption peak intensity of n–σ* transition of N-PDs is lower, and the absorption peak caused by n–π* transition is higher and more obvious, which should be attributed to the clusters agglomeration between N-PDs chromophore. More interestingly, N-PDs have a more unique dual excitation and single emission phenomenon (Fig. 3c). The best excitation and emission wavelengths are (Ex1 = 244 nm, Ex2 = 353 nm; Em = 437 nm). And when the excitation wavelength is in the range of 240 to 420 nm, the emission wavelength is stable at 437 nm (Fig. 3d). The reason may be that N-PDs have a more compact structure than CA-PEI, and the isolated amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl chromophores are closer to these chromophore clusters, which makes most of the energy generated by n–σ* transition be absorbed by the absorption band of the n-π* transition, and the FRET process is more intense. More evidence can be obtained from Fig. 3d. The emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths have some smaller emission peaks at around 300 nm, which should be caused by the unsuccessful transfer of a smaller part of the energy. Significantly, when excited with 244 nm excitation light, N-PDs have a wider Stokes shift (193 nm) than general fluorescent materials, showing a higher anti-interference ability in practical applications. Based on the above analysis, the possible mechanism of dual excitation and monochromatic emission of N-PDs is shown in Fig. 5 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion on the luminescence mechanism of CA-PEI and N-PDs
ce:section.ce:para: CA-PEI and N-PDs contain a large number of primary, secondary and tertiary amines. And many researchers believe that the oxidized tertiary amines are the luminescence center of non-aromatic fluorescent substances. However, in this work, CA-PEI was synthesized under nitrogen protection and low temperature (0 °C) conditions to avoid the oxidation of tertiary amines. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CA-PEI in aqueous medium was 2.30%, and it can emit strong blue fluorescence in solid state, with QY of 7.74%. The QY of N-PDs obtained after hydrothermal treatment of CA-PEI molecules is as high as 18.89%. Therefore, the luminescence mechanism that the oxidation of tertiary ammonia is the luminescence center seems not rigorous, because the oxidation is avoided during the synthesis process, CA-PEI still has strong fluorescence emission. However, the oxidation process can indeed increase the emission efficiency. In addition, more and more non-aromatic fluorescent substances that do not contain amino groups have been gradually reported and researchers have also proposed more luminescence mechanisms, such as amide emission [30], electron transfer between chromophores [28], restriction of molecular rotation and vibration [8, 31] and carbonyl group aggregation [32, 33].

Then, the luminescence mechanism of CA-PEI and N-PDs are further discussed. First, the same preparation process was used to synthesize white solid TA-PEI with tricarboxylic acid (TA) instead of CA. FTIR and 1H NMR results show that there is not much structural difference between CA-PEI and TA-PEI (Figs. S2 and S3). TA-PEI has no fluorescence emission (Fig. S4), it is obviously that the luminescence mechanisms such as amide luminescence and restriction of molecular rotation and vibration are not reasonable here. It is worth noting that the difference between CA-PEI and TA-PEI is that the former has an extra hydroxyl functional group. Therefore, the presence of hydroxyl may play an important role in the fluorescence emission process. According to reports, hydroxyl groups contribute to the formation of hydrogen bonds and further improve molecular rigidity, which is beneficial for fluorescence. Some researchers believe that the electron transfer process between hydroxyl groups and fluorescent chromophores will result in a significant increase in fluorescence.

Similarly, we synthesized another polymer dot TA-PEI-PDs by hydrothermal reaction using TA-PEI as raw material, and its QY was only 4.52%. TA-PEI-PDs and N-PDs have the same preparation process and similar micro morphology, but their QY is much lower than that of N-PDs. These data fully prove that the electron transfer between the chromophores involved in the hydroxyl group can greatly enhance the fluorescence emission of N-PDs, but this is not the root cause of the fluorescence emission. Recent research by Yuan and Tang et al. showed that clusters of chromophores such as carbonyl and hydroxyl were the fluorescence emission centers. However, in many works, the three transition modes of π–π* transition, n–σ* transition and n–π* transition can emit fluorescence [34]. Even in simple carbonyl-containing materials (such as formaldehyde, acetone and acrylamide), fluorescence emission can also be observed [2].

We believe that the fluorescence emission of non-aromatic fluorescent materials may be derived from the structure of functional groups containing heteroatoms (i.e. N, O, S, P, etc. containing lone pairs of electrons). Those structures that tend to form hydrogen bonds and supramolecular assembly will lead to clusters or aggregation and then such structural congestion or increased rigidity will significantly increase the intensity of fluorescence emission [2, 35].
In summary, N-PDs have better fluorescence properties than CA-PEI, and the following research work focuses on the properties and application of N-PDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concentration dependence and stability of N-PDs
ce:section.ce:para: First, the influence of N-PDs concentration on absorption, excitation and emission spectra was studied. As shown in Fig. 6 a, as the concentration increases from 1 μg mL-1 to 32 μg mL-1, the absorption peak intensity at 244 nm and 353 nm increases linearly. And when excited at 244 nm and 353 nm, the emission at 437 nm increases non-linearly (Fig. 6b and c). This phenomenon is widespread in non-aromatic fluorescent materials and is generally believed to be caused by the fluorescence inner-filter effect [36]. Interestingly, when the solution concentration increases from 1 μg mL−1 to 4 μg mL−1, the excitation peak intensity at 244 nm is higher than that at 353 nm. When the concentration continues to increase, the excitation peak intensity at 353 nm is higher than that at 244 nm (Fig. 6d). This phenomenon can be explained by the fact that isolated chromophores on the surface of N-PDs aggregate more easily in concentrated solutions.

Then the stability of N-PDs under high sodium ion environment and 365 nm UV lamp irradiation was studied. As shown in Fig. 6e, N-PDs still maintain a fluorescence intensity above 85% under such a high sodium ion environment (0.2 M). Similarly, after irradiating the N-PDs solution with 365 nm UV light for 10 h, the fluorescence intensity of N-PDs still remained above 87%, showing excellent resistance to photobleaching (Fig. 6f). Additionally, when N-PDs are in a solid state or dissolved in an aqueous medium and then stored in a dark environment at 4 °C, they can maintain stability for at least half a year. These data show that N-PDs have excellent stability, which provides prerequisites for application exploration in a variety of fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of N-PDs to copper ion
ce:section.ce:para: N-PDs have the properties of dual excitation and single emission, bright blue fluorescent emission, excellent stability, and water solubility. These excellent properties make it applicable to various fields such as environmental science and biology. Many fluorescent materials using PEI as a precursor have been synthesized, such as carbon dots [37], polymer dots [25, 26], hyperbranched polyamide molecules [38, 39], etc., and most of them have the ability to respond to Cu2+. N-PDs, as a non-aromatic fluorescent material of CA-functionalized PEI has no exception, and Cu2+ can rapidly quench the fluorescence emission of N-PDs. Firstly, the quenching mechanism was explored through the experiments of fluorescence spectrum, absorption spectrum and several molecular aqueous solutions macroscopically responding to Cu2+. From Fig. 7 a, it can be clearly seen that copper ions have obvious absorption only below 260 nm, and there is no absorption in the wavelength band above 260 nm. And the excitation spectrum intensity and emission spectrum intensity (437 nm) of N-PDs excited at 244 nm and 353 nm decreased significantly after adding a sufficient amount of Cu2+ to the N-PDs aqueous solution. It is worth noting that the two absorption bands of N-PDs centered at 244 nm and 353 nm completely disappeared, but two new broad absorption bands centered at 275 nm and 620 nm appeared. According to relevant literature reports [6, 40], the new absorption spectrum may be derived from the copper amine complexes formed between Cu2+ and the PEI part of N-PDs. In order to prove this opinion, we added Cu2+ to the aqueous solutions of CA, EDA, DETA, PEI and N-PDs to observe the macroscopic color change of the solution. And the result is obvious that after adding Cu2+, only the aqueous solution of molecules containing amino groups changed from colorless and transparent to blue or purple, while the CA aqueous solution did not change color. This phenomenon of color changing provides strong evidence for the fact that Cu2+ is compounded with the amino part of N-PDs. In addition, after careful observation, it can be found that these two newly generated absorption bands, respectively, have large overlaps (black circles) with the two characteristic absorption bands and emission spectra of N-PDs. Therefore, we believe that Cu2+ can react with the PEI part of N-PDs to form the copper amine complexes and then quenches the fluorescence of N-PDs through fluorescence inner-filter effect. In addition, the experimental results show that the addition of biothiols (Cys, Hcy, and GSH) and EDTA-Na2 can restore the fluorescence of N-PDs that was quenched by Cu2+ (Fig. 7c), similar to the results of many studies. The fluorescence recovery mechanism involves a stronger binding force between biothiol or ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid and Cu2+, which can peel off the Cu2+ on the surface of N-PDs to restore the fluorescence. Therefore, the fluorescence of N-PDs is reversible, which means that it has the potential as an excellent Cu2+ sensor.

Before proceeding with ion detection, determining the optimal solution pH and response time is critical to the detection effect. As shown in Fig. 8 a, when the pH is 5.0, the maximum fluorescence intensity can be obtained. Both lowering and raising pH will reduce the fluorescence emission of N-PDs. According to literatures [6, 37, 41], it is speculated that the change in fluorescence is due to the combined effects of the protonation and deprotonation of the hydroxyl groups and the amino groups. As pH of the solution changes from 5.0 to 1.0, the obvious red shift of both absorption and fluorescence peaks can only be attributed to the protonation of the hydroxyl groups (Fig. S5) [6]. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 8b, the fluorescence of N-PDs aqueous solution decreased rapidly after adding Cu2+and remained stable within 2 min. Therefore, 5.0 was selected as the detecting pH value, and the response time was set to 2 min.

In practical applications, it is of great importance to assess the selectivity, competitiveness and sensitivity of N-PDs in response to Cu2+. As clearly shown in Fig. 8c, when different ions were added into the N-PDs solution, only Cu2+ can significantly quench the fluorescence of N-PDs, while the response of N-PDs and Cu2+ was not affected by other interfering ions. Fig. 8d shows the fluorescence spectra of Cu2+ with different concentrations (0-12 μmol L−1) added to the N-PDs solution. The fluorescence intensity of N-PDs decreases rapidly with the increase of Cu2+ concentration. And as the concentration of Cu2+ increases to 10 μmol L−1, the fluorescence intensity of N-PDs decreased to nearly 1/10 and gradually stabilized (Fig. 8e). Significantly, there is an excellent semi-logarithmic relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency and the concentration of Cu2+ (Fig. 8f) when the concentration of Cu2+ is in the range of 0.1–8.0 μmol L−1, and the equation is Log (I 0 / I) = 0.09817 C – 0.01097 (R 2 = 0.9988, n = 10), where I 0 and I represent the fluorescence intensity of N-PDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively, while C represents the concentration of Cu2+. The calculated limit of detection (LOD) is 2.5 nmol L−1 [40], which is much lower than that of some other polymer dots, quantum dots, and organic conjugated fluorescent materials (Table 1 ). Lower LOD represents better analytical performance and higher sensitivity, indicating that N-PDs can be used as an excellent sensor to achieve high-sensitive determination of Cu2+. As mentioned above, the reason why the LOD is so low may be because N-PDs contain a large number of amino groups and are easy to combine with Cu2+ to form copper amine complexes. And this kind of complex is produced from the inside or surface of N-PDs, and it is very easy to quench its fluorescence emission through fluorescence inner-filter effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of copper ions in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further explore the practical performance of N-PDs, this method is used to analyze actual water samples by testing the river water obtained from the “Jiazi Lake” of University of Jinan. As shown in Table S2, the recovery rate of Cu2+ is between 99% and 105%. In addition, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of Cu2+ is in the range of 0.9–1.7%. These results indicate that N-PDs can detect Cu2+ in real samples.

In daily life, some unscrupulous vendors produce various counterfeit and shoddy goods for huge profits. Therefore, it is particularly important to develop better anti-counterfeiting encryption technology. N-PDs have extremely excellent fluorescence properties and high fluorescence intensity, which attracts us to explore their applications in invisible anti-counterfeiting inks. First, the N-PDs aqueous solution with a concentration of 10 mg/mL is directly injected into a clean pen without any chemical doping modification. The ink in the pen is colorless and transparent under sunlight, however, it emits bright blue fluorescence under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (Fig. 9 a). As shown in Fig. 9b, the words “University of Jinan” written on the paper with this pen are invisible in sunlight, but can display bright blue fluorescent writing under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp.

It is known that the fluorescence of N-PDs can be quenched by Cu2+, and EDTA-Na2 can efficiently restore the fluorescence of N-PDs. Based on this mechanism, the encryption and decryption process of N-PDs ink has been further studied. As shown in Fig. 9c, the aqueous solution of Cu2+ (1 mol L−1) was dropped on the English words, and the English words are hidden. After dropping the EDTA-Na2 aqueous solution on the encrypted words, the fluorescence gradually recovered to a level that can be clearly recognized within one minute. This encryption and decryption process further improve the security level. In addition, the water-soluble N-PDs are transparent, non-polluting, easy to prepare and clean, making them a wide range of application potential.

All in all, we synthesized two novel non-aromatic fluorescent materials, CA-PEI and N-PDs. Their structures and fluorescence properties were determined by NMR, FTIR, GPC, XPS, fluorescence spectroscopy and absorption spectroscopy. The principles of dual excitation and dual emission of CA-PEI and dual excitation and single emission of N-PDs were analyzed, respectively. The ability and response mechanism of N-PDs to detect Cu2+ in the water phase were also studied. Cu2+ can combine with PEI part of N-PDs to form copper amine complexes and then quench the fluorescence emission of N-PDs through fluorescence inner-filter effect. Finally, using N-PDs as fluorescent inks in the field of anti-counterfeiting has achieved satisfactory results.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21477047, 22076063) and Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2017MEM006).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.ceja.2020.100046.
